suggested_policies.rationale: Holden already covers wildcard actions (HLD_AWS_142) and wildcard principals (HLD_AWS_143) on data.aws_iam_policy_document statements, but has no equivalent for wildcard resources. checkov's CKV_AWS_356 covers exactly this third dimension. A statement with effect=Allow and resources containing a literal "*" grants the listed actions against every resource in the account regardless of how tightly actions/principals are scoped, so this closes the triangle with a check of identical shape and rigor to its two siblings.
suggested_policies.yaml:
id: HLD_PROPOSED_002
name: IAM policy document Allow statements must not use a wildcard resource
principle: identity-and-access
severity: HIGH
rationale: |
  An IAM policy statement with effect = "Allow" and resources = ["*"] grants the
  listed actions against every resource in the account, not just the resource(s)
  the statement was written to protect. Even when actions and principals are
  tightly scoped, a wildcard resource means any future action added to the
  statement automatically applies account-wide. This is the same class of risk
  as a wildcard action (HLD_AWS_142) or wildcard principal (HLD_AWS_143), just on
  the third axis of an IAM statement, and is a common copy-paste mistake from
  example policies.
message: |
  data.aws_iam_policy_document statement has effect = "Allow" and resources
  contains a literal wildcard "*", granting the listed actions on every
  resource in the account.
remediation: |
  Replace the wildcard with the specific resource ARN(s) the statement should
  apply to:

    data "aws_iam_policy_document" "example" {
      statement {
        effect    = "Allow"
        actions   = ["s3:GetObject"]
        resources = ["arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*"]
      }
    }

  If the statement is genuinely intended to apply account-wide (rare), suppress
  this finding with a holden suppression annotation and document why.
resources:
  data.aws_iam_policy_document:
    path: statement
    not_any:
      all:
        - any:
            - path: effect
              absent: true
            - path: effect
              equals: Allow
        - path: resources
          contains: "*"

suggested_policies.rationale: aws_api_gateway_method_settings has zero entries in coverage_by_type today. checkov runs three checks against it (CKV_AWS_225 caching enabled, CKV_AWS_308 caching encrypted, CKV_AWS_276 data trace disabled); the data-trace one is the security-relevant one worth porting first, since data_trace_enabled = true writes full request/response payloads (including headers and bodies, which may contain credentials or PII) into CloudWatch Logs, and it's easy to leave on after a debugging session. The caching checks are cost/reliability concerns better suited to a follow-up if wanted.
suggested_policies.yaml:
id: HLD_PROPOSED_003
name: API Gateway method settings must not enable data trace logging
principle: data-protection
severity: MEDIUM
rationale: |
  Setting data_trace_enabled = true in an aws_api_gateway_method_settings
  settings block causes API Gateway to write the full request and response
  payload, including headers and body, to CloudWatch Logs for every matching
  method call. This routinely captures authentication tokens, PII, or other
  sensitive data that the application never intended to persist in logs, and
  is most often left on after a debugging session rather than a deliberate
  choice. CloudWatch Logs have separate (often weaker) access controls than
  the application itself, widening the blast radius of a logs-access compromise.
message: |
  aws_api_gateway_method_settings has settings.data_trace_enabled = true,
  causing full request/response payloads to be written to CloudWatch Logs.
remediation: |
  Disable data trace logging (or omit the attribute, since it defaults to
  false) and rely on access logging / method-level metrics instead:

    resource "aws_api_gateway_method_settings" "example" {
      rest_api_id = aws_api_gateway_rest_api.example.id
      stage_name  = aws_api_gateway_stage.example.stage_name
      method_path = "*/*"

      settings {
        data_trace_enabled = false
        logging_level       = "ERROR"
      }
    }
resources:
  aws_api_gateway_method_settings:
    path: settings.data_trace_enabled
    not_equals: true

suggested_policies.rationale: aws_api_gateway_method's only holden coverage is HLD_AWS_193 (authorization). checkov's CKV2_AWS_53 separately requires a request_validator_id, since API Gateway request validation is the first line of defense against malformed or oversized payloads reaching the backend integration (Lambda, HTTP, etc.) and is independent of authentication/authorization. A method can be perfectly authenticated and still forward unvalidated garbage to the backend.
suggested_policies.yaml:
id: HLD_PROPOSED_004
name: API Gateway methods should have a request validator attached
principle: governance
severity: MEDIUM
rationale: |
  An aws_api_gateway_method with no request_validator_id forwards whatever the
  caller sends straight to the backend integration with no schema, parameter,
  or body validation at the gateway layer. This pushes all input validation
  onto the backend (or skips it entirely), increasing the attack surface for
  malformed payloads, oversized requests, and injection attempts that a
  gateway-level validator would reject before they ever reach application code.
message: |
  aws_api_gateway_method does not set request_validator_id, so no request
  validation happens at the gateway before the backend integration is invoked.
remediation: |
  Attach a request validator to the method:

    resource "aws_api_gateway_request_validator" "example" {
      name                        = "validate-requests"
      rest_api_id                 = aws_api_gateway_rest_api.example.id
      validate_request_body       = true
      validate_request_parameters = true
    }

    resource "aws_api_gateway_method" "example" {
      rest_api_id          = aws_api_gateway_rest_api.example.id
      resource_id          = aws_api_gateway_resource.example.id
      http_method          = "POST"
      authorization        = "AWS_IAM"
      request_validator_id = aws_api_gateway_request_validator.example.id
    }
resources:
  aws_api_gateway_method:
    path: request_validator_id
    not_null: true

suggested_policies.rationale: HLD_AWS_134 already requires that every aws_wafv2_web_acl has a companion aws_wafv2_web_acl_association, but that only catches an orphaned WAF ACL — it says nothing if no WAF ACL was ever created at all. checkov's CKV2_AWS_29 checks the other direction: that a public API Gateway stage is actually fronted by a WAF. coverage_by_type["aws_api_gateway_stage"] has no WAF-related entry, so this is a genuine gap, complementary rather than duplicate to HLD_AWS_134 (different source resource type in the requires_companion check). Scored MEDIUM rather than HIGH because private/regional APIs using an alternative compensating control (e.g. an IP-restricted resource policy, as in the module this was found against) are a legitimate exception, not just an oversight.
suggested_policies.yaml:
id: HLD_PROPOSED_005
name: API Gateway stages should be associated with a WAFv2 web ACL
principle: network-security
severity: MEDIUM
rationale: |
  An API Gateway stage with no aws_wafv2_web_acl_association has no protection
  against SQL injection, XSS, bad bots, or volumetric abuse at the edge —
  every request reaches the method/integration layer unfiltered. This is the
  mirror image of HLD_AWS_134 (which catches a WAF ACL with no association):
  here the stage has no WAF at all. Regional or private APIs that rely on an
  alternative compensating control (IAM authorization, an IP-restricted
  resource policy, mTLS) are a legitimate exception and should suppress this
  finding explicitly rather than silently pass it.
message: |
  aws_api_gateway_stage has no companion aws_wafv2_web_acl_association, so no
  WAFv2 web ACL is protecting this stage.
remediation: |
  Associate a WAFv2 web ACL with the stage:

    resource "aws_wafv2_web_acl_association" "example" {
      resource_arn = aws_api_gateway_stage.example.arn
      web_acl_arn  = aws_wafv2_web_acl.example.arn
    }

  If this stage is intentionally protected by a different control (IAM auth,
  IP-restricted resource policy, mTLS), suppress this finding with a holden
  suppression annotation documenting which control substitutes for the WAF.
resources:
  aws_api_gateway_stage:
    requires_companion:
      type: aws_wafv2_web_acl_association
      via: resource_arn

suggested_policies.rationale: HLD_TF_041 already establishes the pattern of checking lifecycle.<attribute> across multiple resource types (there for prevent_destroy on databases/keys/tables). aws_api_gateway_deployment and aws_api_gateway_rest_api have zero holden coverage today, and checkov has a dedicated check for each (CKV_AWS_217, CKV_AWS_237) verifying lifecycle.create_before_destroy = true. Without it, any change forcing replacement of the deployment or REST API destroys the live API before the replacement exists, causing an availability gap — exactly the scenario this module's own root module already guards against on both resources.
suggested_policies.yaml:
id: HLD_PROPOSED_006
name: API Gateway deployments and REST APIs must enable lifecycle.create_before_destroy
principle: reliability
severity: MEDIUM
rationale: |
  aws_api_gateway_deployment and aws_api_gateway_rest_api are destroy-and-recreate
  by default when a change forces replacement (e.g. certain endpoint_configuration
  or body changes). Without lifecycle.create_before_destroy = true, Terraform
  destroys the live deployment or REST API before creating its replacement,
  causing a hard outage for every client of the API for the duration of the
  apply. create_before_destroy makes the replacement resource first, so
  traffic only cuts over once the new one exists.
message: |
  aws_api_gateway_deployment or aws_api_gateway_rest_api does not set
  lifecycle.create_before_destroy = true, so a forced replacement will destroy
  the live resource before its replacement is created.
remediation: |
  Add a lifecycle block to the resource:

    resource "aws_api_gateway_rest_api" "example" {
      name = "example"

      lifecycle {
        create_before_destroy = true
      }
    }
resources:
  aws_api_gateway_deployment:
    all:
      - path: lifecycle.create_before_destroy
        not_null: true
      - path: lifecycle.create_before_destroy
        equals: true
  aws_api_gateway_rest_api:
    all:
      - path: lifecycle.create_before_destroy
        not_null: true
      - path: lifecycle.create_before_destroy
        equals: true
